relevant_files.role: Main SecurityScanner implementation using regex-based line-by-line scanning
PatternEngine with compiled regex patterns from embedded JSON
14 regex pattern definitions for vulnerability detection
Core types: Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence, ValidatedFinding
Security module exports and submodule organization
Integration tests including multi-line limitation test from PR #736
Crate dependencies - currently no tree-sitter deps

conventions.code_style: Rust with SPDX license headers, comprehensive doc comments
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for libraries, anyhow for applications
conventions.testing: Unit tests in source files, integration tests in tests/ directory
conventions.patterns: LazyLock for global singletons, builder pattern with bon
conventions.serialization: serde with rename_all lowercase for enums
conventions.architecture: Modular security subsystem with clear separation of concerns
conventions.commits: Conventional Commits format, GPG signed, DCO sign-off required

patterns.impact: Cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities where source and sink are separated

patterns.context: patterns.json included at compile time via include_str!
patterns.impact: Patterns are static; runtime customization requires code changes

patterns.context: Patterns can specify applicable file extensions
patterns.impact: Reduces false positives but requires per-language patterns
Pattern compilation happens once at first use

patterns.context: scan_diff() parses unified diff format, only scans added lines
patterns.impact: Focuses on new code changes, ignores removed vulnerabilities

anti_patterns.example: SQL injection where user_input is assigned on line 1, used in query on line 3

anti_patterns.issue: No runtime pattern loading or customization without recompilation
anti_patterns.alternative: Consider external pattern files or database

related_issues.summary: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection - Main feature request to enable multi-line vulnerability detection via AST parsing
test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation - Documents known regex scanner limitation with explicit test case
PR: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation - Merged PR adding test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected

constraints: No breaking changes to existing SecurityScanner API
14 existing regex patterns must be migrated or supported
Must support Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, PHP based on existing patterns
Binary size concerns from adding grammar crates
Performance: incremental parsing for large files
Hybrid approach preferred per issue #737 - keep regex for simple patterns
test_coverage: Strong test coverage with unit tests in scanner.rs and patterns.rs, integration tests in security_integration.rs. Tests cover: pattern detection, file extension filtering, diff parsing, line number accuracy, ignored paths. Notable test: test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected explicitly documents current limitation.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter
library_findings.role: Core AST parsing library - creates parse trees from source code
library_findings.notes: Version 0.24 recommended. Provides incremental parsing, language-agnostic core.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-rust
library_findings.role: Rust grammar for tree-sitter - enables Rust AST parsing
library_findings.notes: Version 0.23 recommended. Required for scanning Rust files with AST.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-python
library_findings.role: Python grammar - enables Python AST parsing
library_findings.notes: Required for SQL injection detection in Python code.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-javascript
library_findings.notes: Required for XSS detection in JS/TS files.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-java
library_findings.notes: Required for SQL injection and XXE detection in Java.

library_findings.name: regex
library_findings.role: Current pattern matching engine - will remain for simple patterns
library_findings.notes: Keep for simple line-based patterns to avoid overhead of full AST parsing.

approaches.title: Approach 1: AST-Only Migration (Full Replacement)
approaches.summary: Replace regex-based PatternEngine entirely with tree-sitter AST parsing. All 14 patterns rewritten as tree-sitter queries. Single AST-based scanner handles all detection.
pros: Maximum detection capability for multi-line vulnerabilities
Language-aware parsing eliminates many false positives
Unified detection approach - no hybrid complexity
Tree-sitter queries are more expressive than regex for code patterns
cons: Major breaking change - full rewrite of PatternEngine
All 14 patterns need complete rewrite in Scheme-like query syntax
Binary size increase from 5-10 grammar crates (~2-5MB estimated)
Performance overhead for simple patterns that regex handled efficiently
files_to_touch: crates/aptu-core/src/security/patterns.rs - Complete rewrite to use tree-sitter
crates/aptu-core/src/security/scanner.rs - Minor updates for AST context
crates/aptu-core/tests/security_integration.rs - Update tests for new detection behavior

approaches.title: Approach 2: Hybrid Architecture (Recommended by Issue #737)
approaches.summary: Keep regex PatternEngine for simple line-based patterns, add new AstPatternEngine for multi-line detection. Scanner uses both engines, merges results. Tree-sitter only for patterns needing AST context.
pros: Incremental adoption - no breaking changes to existing patterns
Performance: regex for simple patterns, AST only when needed
Lower risk - can validate tree-sitter approach before full commitment
Aligns with suggested approach in issue #737
Smaller binary size - only include grammars for languages needing AST
cons: Potential duplicate findings from both engines (requires deduplication)
files_to_touch: crates/aptu-core/src/security/scanner.rs - Integrate both engines, merge findings
crates/aptu-core/Cargo.toml - Add tree-sitter + grammar deps (optional features)

approaches.title: Approach 3: Data-Flow Analysis Layer on Top of AST
approaches.summary: Build a higher-level data-flow/taint analysis engine that uses tree-sitter AST as foundation. Tracks variables from source (user input) to sink (dangerous function). Most sophisticated but also most complex.
pros: Detects complex vulnerabilities: SQL injection, command injection, XSS with flow analysis
Can track user input through multiple assignments and function calls
Industry-standard approach used by commercial SAST tools (Semgrep, CodeQL)
Future-proof foundation for advanced security features
cons: Very high complexity - requires building taint tracking system
Requires deep understanding of each language's semantics
May need custom logic beyond tree-sitter (control flow analysis)
Overkill for current 14 pattern use case
files_to_touch: Multiple new modules for control flow, call graph analysis

recommendation:
Recommend Approach 2: Hybrid Architecture. This aligns with the suggested approach in issue #737 and balances multiple concerns:

1. **Risk Mitigation**: Keeps existing regex engine working, no breaking changes
2. **Incremental Value**: Can implement tree-sitter for just SQL injection first (the most impactful multi-line pattern), then expand
3. **Performance**: Simple patterns (hardcoded secrets, weak crypto) stay fast with regex; only complex multi-line patterns use AST
4. **Maintainability**: Pattern authors can choose appropriate engine per pattern
5. **Binary Size**: Use Cargo features to make grammars optional (e.g., feature = "ast-rust", "ast-python")

Implementation roadmap:
- Phase 1: Add tree-sitter-rust, implement SQL injection pattern as proof of concept
- Phase 2: Evaluate detection quality vs regex on real-world code
- Phase 3: Add remaining language grammars and migrate patterns that benefit from AST

Key files to create/modify:
- `ast_engine.rs` - New tree-sitter integration
- Update `scanner.rs` to use both engines
- Add feature-gated grammar dependencies in Cargo.toml
